title: Needle-felted stoat
subtitle: A slim chestnut stoat with white underparts and alert black eyes in merino
excerpt: Needle-felt a slender stoat in warm chestnut and white merino, using a wire armature to hold the long sinuous body and arched posture. The finished piece is about 14 cm long.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. Mustelid forms are an intermediate British needle-felting subject; the elongated body is best built on a wire armature.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres together without heat or water.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon; the standard starting material for needle felting.

content.text: is built around a wire armature that gives the long sinuous body its shape and allows the distinctive arched posture. Without wire the body would need to lie flat. The chestnut
for the upper coat covers the armature completely; the white underside is the last layer to go on.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees onto the foam mat only. Wear a finger guard on the supporting hand.

items.name: Merino roving, warm chestnut or russet (upper coat)
Merino roving, white or cream (underside, chin)

content.text: Twist two 30 cm lengths of armature wire together for the central spine. Bend the combined wire into a rough stoat shape: the body forms a gentle S-curve when viewed from the side, the back end forms a tail, and two short offshoots at the shoulder and hip become stub legs.

Wrap a thin layer of chestnut roving tightly around the wire spine, binding it at intervals by stabbing through the roving into itself with the size-38 needle. Cover the entire wire including the tail. The diameter should be about 1.5 cm at the body and taper to 0.5 cm at the tail tip.
Step 3: Build up the body shape

Add more chestnut roving over the shoulder and hip areas to give the body a slight swell. Stab firmly to compact each addition. The stoat body is thicker behind the shoulders than at the waist. Build the head as a separate egg shape about 2.5 cm long, felt it firm, then needle it to the front of the neck.

Turn the stoat over and apply white roving to the throat, chest, and belly. The boundary between chestnut and white runs along each flank in a clean, fairly straight line. Use the size-40 needle to keep the boundary sharp. Apply a small white patch to the chin.
Step 5: Add the black tail tip and eyes

Apply a 1 cm section of black roving to the tail tip and stab it firmly. Roll two small black balls for the eyes, about 4 mm each. Felt each one until very firm, then position them on the sides of the head, slightly forward.

items.problem: The long body droops or kinks
items.cause: The wire gauge was too thin or the roving wrap was not firm enough
items.fix: Use heavier gauge wire (2.5 mm) or add a second length of the same wire, twisted together with the first. Wrap the wire more firmly before building up the outer coat.

items.problem: The white underside boundary is ragged
items.cause: The roving was applied too thickly in one pass
items.fix: Apply the white in two thin layers. Use the size-40 needle at the flank boundary and work slowly.
